title: Scaling Laws for Neural Machine Translation

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - SCALING LAWS FOR NEURAL MACHINE TRANSLA TION

## **Behrooz Ghorbani** _[∗]_ **, Orhan Firat, Markus Freitag, Ankur Bapna, Maxim Krikun, Xavier Garcia, Ciprian Chelba & Colin Cherry** Google AI

## ABSTRACT

We present an empirical study of the scaling properties of encoder-decoder Transformer models used in neural machine translation (NMT). We show that crossentropy loss as a function of model size follows a certain scaling law. Specifically (i) We propose a formula which describes the scaling behavior of cross-entropy loss as a bivariate function of encoder and decoder size, and show that it gives accurate predictions under a variety of scaling approaches and languages; we show that the total number of parameters alone is not sufficient for such purposes. (ii) We observe different power law exponents when scaling the decoder vs the encoder, and provide recommendations for optimal allocation of encoder/decoder capacity based on this observation. (iii) We also report that the scaling behavior of the model is acutely influenced by _composition bias_ of the train/test sets, which we define as any deviation from naturally generated text (either via machine generated or human translated text). We observe that natural text on the target side enjoys scaling, which manifests as successful reduction of the cross-entropy loss. (iv) Finally, we investigate the relationship between the model size, cross-entropy loss and the quality of the generated translations. We find two different behaviors, depending on the nature of the test data. For test sets which were originally translated from target language to source language, both loss and BLEU score improve as model size increases. In contrast, for test sets originally translated from source language to target language, the loss improves, but the BLEU score stops improving after a certain threshold. We release generated text from all models used in this study.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Scaling properties of neural networks have long been an intriguing topic of study (Ahmad & Tesauro, 1988; Amari et al., 1992). Along with the practical success of modern neural networks at scale, theoretical understanding of the factors governing the quality and training dynamics of large neural networks has also being developing (Advani et al., 2020; Rosenfeld et al., 2019; Geiger et al., 2020; Ghorbani et al., 2021; Cho, 2020; Hutter, 2021; Bahri et al., 2021; Loureiro et al., 2021). In particular, scaling model sizes, datasets and the total computation budget has been identified as a reliable approach to improve generalization performance on several machine learning tasks. For many of these tasks the scaling behavior of neural networks is highly predictable; model fit or test loss can be described precisely as a function of its number of parameters (Hestness et al., 2017; Kaplan et al., 2020; Henighan et al., 2020; Hernandez et al., 2021; Rosenfeld et al., 2019). Neural machine translation (NMT) has long enjoyed the benefits of scaling (Huang et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Lepikhin et al., 2021), but studies investigating the scaling behavior of NMT models are missing. We present a large-scale systematic study of scaling laws for encoder-decoder Transformer models applied to NMT (Vaswani et al., 2017).

We start with highlighting the major differences between decoder-only language models, where the majority of the previous work has focused, and encoder-decoder (conditional) language models applied to NMT. The two differ along a few crucial dimensions. The first difference results from the very nature of the separate architectures being used, i.e. decoder-only vs encoder-decoder. The presence of separate architectural components complicates the study of scaling properties due to the

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

increased degree of freedom. Second, contrary to language modeling, the task of machine translation is conditional: the task is predictive rather than fully generative. Furthermore, this prediction task is ambiguous: there is no one right answer for a given source, and translations can vary substantially depending on the translator’s incentives. This manifests itself as different scaling benefits for different test sets. To take an extreme example, a test set translated by someone who writes nearly wordfor-word translations may benefit less from model scaling than one translated by someone who considers each translation a work of art. In this work, these differences in difficulty coincide with the translation direction of the test set; that is, whether the source was translated into the target (source-original) or vice versa (target-original). Source-original data has translated text on the target side, which contains several artifacts of “translationese” that distinguish it from text originally written in that language, often lacking the diversity and complexity of “natural” text (Koppel & Ordan, 2011), while target-original data requires the prediction of more complex natural text on the target side. Finally, unlike language models, NMT is evaluated on metrics that quantify generation quality against reference translations (for eg. BLEU) (Papineni et al., 2002) instead of evaluating model fit (perplexity) on an evaluation set.

In this paper, we aim to provide empirical answers to the following research questions:

1. **Does the encoder-decoder architecture for NMT share the same scaling law function as language models?** Contrary to previous work on LM, we show that a univariate law depending on the total number of parameters in the network does not adequately describe the scaling behavior of NMT models. Our scaling laws parameterize the cross entropy loss as a bivariate function of the number of encoder parameters and the number of decoder parameters as separate variables. Our results indicate that the scaling behavior is largely determined by the total capacity of the model, and the capacity allocation between the encoder and the decoder.

2. **How does the naturalness of source/target side data affect scaling behavior?** We study the effect of naturalness of the source and the target text, both for training and evaluation. When evaluating with target side natural text, scaling the model capacity continues improving model quality throughout our range of measurements. On the other hand, improvements on cross-entropy saturate (or reaches the irreducible error region) on source side natural evaluation sets even for moderately-sized models.

3. **Do scaling improvements in cross-entropy translate into corresponding improvements in generation quality?** Finally we study the relationship between generation quality and cross-entropy and how their correlation changes as we: (i) Scale different components of the model (encoder vs decoder) and (ii) Evaluate on source-natural or target-natural test sets.

Our results on multiple language pairs and training/test data compositions validate that **model scaling predictably improves the cross-entropy on validation data** . However, our findings also raise several questions regarding the effect of naturalness of training and evaluation text and how cross-entropy eventually relates with generation quality for auto-regressive generative models.[1]

- 2 EFFECT OF SCALING ON CROSS-ENTROPY

- 2.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETTING

**Model Architectures and Training** We train a series of pre-layer norm Transformer networks with varying sizes (Xiong et al., 2020). Models are trained with per-token cross-entropy loss and Adafactor optimizer (Shazeer & Stern, 2018). All models are trained with a fixed batch-size of 500k tokens and dropout rate of 0 _._ 1 for residuals, feed-forward activations and attention. All models are trained to near convergence for 500k training steps. Details of the model hyper-parameters are described in Appendix C.
**Model Scaling** Transformer architecture consists of Transformer Blocks: a cascade of self-attention, cross-attention and feed-forward layers, each having multiple adjustable hyper-parameters (e.g.

> 1A few weeks before the publication of this manuscript on Arxiv, Gordon et al. (2021) appeared on OpenReview. While both papers study scaling laws for NMT, our studies focus on different parameter regimes (393K-56M vs 100M-4B). See Appendix A for a comparison of the results.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

model-dimension, number of attention heads, attention projection dimension etc.). Considering the combinatorial expansion of the search space for scaling each one, in this study we choose to vary only the total number of Transformer Blocks, while keeping the internal hyper-parameters intact across different scales. In other words, we scale the depth of the Transformers while keeping width and other variables fixed.[2] We use GPipe pipeline parallelism for our experiments (Huang et al., 2019).

In an encoder-decoder Transformer architecture for NMT, depth scaling can naturally be implemented by varying encoder-decoder blocks independently or symmetrically. Hence, we examine the change in the cross-entropy loss as the number of parameters increase with three depth scaling approaches:

_Encoder Scaling_ : vary encoder depth (2 to 64) while decoder depth is fixed (6 layers).

_Decoder Scaling_ : vary decoder depth (2 to 64) while encoder depth is fixed (6 layers).
_Symmetric Scaling_ : increasing decoder and encoder layers together (from 2 to 64), i.e. the number of Transformer Blocks in the encoder and decoder being equal.

For all experiments, configuration of the individual layers is unchanged: the model dimension, width of the feed-forward layer, and number of attention heads are fixed respectively at 1024, 8192, and 16. 3 Each encoder layer adds approximately 20M parameters to the model while each decoder layer adds around 25M parameters. We train 200 such models which scale the model size by approximately a factor of 50 (from 80M parameters to 4B parameters). Following the convention, we do not count the parameters in the embedding and softmax layers towards the model size. We focus our study on large-scale models: our smallest models require 200 TPUv3 days to train to convergence while our largest models require 2700 TPUv3 days of training.

**Language Pairs** We report results on four language pairs: English _→_ German, German _→_ English, English _→_ Chinese, and Chinese _→_ English. We use in-house web-crawled training datasets with around 2 _._ 2 billion sentence pairs (approximately 55 billion tokens) for English _↔_ German and 781 million sentence pairs for English _↔_ Chinese.

**Evaluation Sets** We use a variety of test sets for evaluation covering different domains: (i) WebDomain (ii) News-Domain (iii) Wikipedia (iv) Patents. The news-domain test sets come from the WMT2019 (Barrault et al., 2019) evaluation campaign (newstest2019) for all language pairs. The other test sets are internal test sets representing the different domains, ranging from 500 to 5000 sentence pairs. For each domain, we randomly sample sentences in the source language and use professional translators to generate a reference translation in the target language. Throughout the paper, we will refer this type of test sets as _source-original_ as the source sentences have been crawled from the web while the reference translations are added later. For most of the domains, we also have a _target-original_ counterpart which is generated in the opposite direction: Sentences are crawled in the target language and human translated into the source language. Earlier work (Freitag et al., 2019; 2020; Graham et al., 2020) showed that it is important to differentiate between the two different kinds of test sets as the style of natural sentences and human (or machine) translations (translationese) is quite different. Cross-entropy loss is evaluated on the different test sets during training. To reduce the variation caused by the parameter fluctuations at the end of the training, we present the median loss over the last 50k steps of the training as the final loss.

## 2.2 RESULTS

Figure 1 shows the empirical evolution of the test loss on the Web-Domain test sets for encoder and decoder scaling for English _→_ German. To compare the empirical results with the scaling laws present in the literature for decoder only models (Kaplan et al., 2020; Henighan et al., 2020), we have fitted a power law of the form _L_[ˆ] ( _N_ ) = _αN[−][p]_ + _L∞_ to the data.[4] Here, _N_ is the total number of parameters outside of embedding / softmax layers and _{α, p, L∞}_ are fitted parameters. As Figure 1 suggests, scaling the encoder has different effects on the test loss compared to scaling the decoder. As such, simple power-laws that only consider the total parameter count, fail to capture the correct scaling behavior of the model.

As such, traditional scaling laws are unable to capture the correct scaling behavior. R-squared (100 _×_[ex][p] total variance[lained variance] ) and maximum absolute deviation ( _∥· ∥∞_ ) are reported for each fit.

**Proposed Scaling Law** To tackle this issue, we present a new scaling law that reflects the encoderdecoder nature of the architecture as well as the bilingual format of the data. Let _Ne_ and _Nd_ be the number of non-embedding parameters in the encoder and the decoder respectively. Then, our proposed scaling law has the form

where _{α, pe, pd, L∞}_ are test set specific (fitted) parameters. In this formulation, _α_ corresponds to the maximum loss reduction that one can hope from scaling, while _pe_ and _pd_ are the scaling exponents for encoder and decoder respectively. _L∞_ corresponds to the irreducible loss of the data.

In Figure 2, we fit our scaling law to the empirical loss values achieved by encoder/decoder scaling models on Web-Domain test sets. The results suggest that our scaling law almost fully captures the variation in the data ( _R_[2] _>_ 99%). In particular, in contrast to traditional scaling laws, our proposed formulation is able to capture the different scaling behaviors of encoder and decoder scaled models.

While these improved fits are encouraging, to truly show that a candidate scaling law captures the model scaling behavior in NMT, it is necessary to verify the out-of-sample prediction power of the law. We provide two such verifications; we compare the predictions of the scaling law fitted on encoder / decoder scaling models with the empirical loss values from (i) symmetrically scaled models, (ii) models randomly sampled from the 2D grid of all potential encoder-decoder configurations.[5] Symmetrically scaled models are chosen for this verification as they are widely used by the NLP community (Raffel et al., 2020). Randomly sampled models are chosen to ensure that our results are not biased towards any particular model class.

Figures 3 & 4 present the out-of-sample results. As the plots suggest, the predictions of the scaling law match the empirical results with remarkable accuracy. These results suggest that Eq. (1) is able to capture the model scaling behavior across different scaling approaches and model sizes.

To ensure that our results generalize across different language pairs, we examine the fit of our scaling law on encoder / decoder scaling models trained on German _→_ English (De _→_ En), Chinese-to-English (Zh _→_ En), and English-to-Chinese (En _→_ Zh) translation tasks. Figure 5 presents the results evaluated on in-domain held-out data. Similar to the En _→_ De case, our proposed functional form is able to closely capture the scaling behavior of these models.

To keep the scope of our experiments manageable, we focus on depth scaling in our analysis. In Appendix H, we ran large-scale experiments to see if other scaling approaches show significant deviations from the trends identified here. For large-scale models, which are the focus of this study, we observe that different scaling approaches yield very similar results: the final achieved test loss across different scaling approaches shows just 1 _−_ 2% variation. We observed that for small models ( _<_ 100M parameters), this variation tends to grow, suggesting that model shape plays a key role in the performance of small models. We leave further analysis of this phenomenon to future research.

## 2.3 ANALYSIS

The above results suggest that the scaling law formalized in Eq. (1) captures the scaling behavior of the Transformer NMT models in multiple language pairs. As such, we can study the fitted coefficients to fully understand the scaling properties of these models. Several observations are in order:

**Decoder vs Encoder Scaling:** On all our test sets, the decoder exponents were observed to be larger than the encoder exponents, _pd > pe_ (see Figure 9). As a result, when improving the test loss is concerned, it is much more effective to scale the decoder rather than the encoder. This is contrary to the usual practice; due to latency considerations, many practitioners train NMT models with deep encoders and shallow decoders (Kasai et al., 2021). Our results suggest this practice could be sub-optimal in terms of loss reduction. Proposition 1 below provides guidance on how to allocate parameters in between the encoder and decoder optimally. ## 3 EFFECT OF DATASET COMPOSITION BIAS ON SCALING BEHAVIOR

Translation deals with the problem of mapping a sequence in one language into another language. A good translation should not only be adequate and fluent, but should ideally also adopt the style of a sentence naturally written in the target language. This necessitates MT models to make sense of natural looking inputs and generate natural looking outputs. As mentioned in Section 2, the examples used to train or test NMT models carry a critical bias, which we refer to as _composition bias_ . Composition bias is introduced because of the unavailability of source-target examples (pairs) that are both natural in the accessible data generating distribution. For any given naturally generated text in a language, the corresponding text in the other language is either translated by humans, introducing _translationese_ bias or translated by other machine translation systems, introducing _MT_ bias. We consider both biases affecting the problem from a similar angle, hence we bundle them and call it composition bias. While machine translation by design has composition bias in the training/test sets employed (Freitag et al., 2020; Riley et al., 2020), its effect on model scaling is unknown. In this section we investigate the role of composition bias in scaling and identify critical factors playing a role.

We caution the reader to not take the composition bias as a problem specific to NMT. In fact as most training corpora in NMT are web-crawled, they can contain machine translation output on either the source or target side. Considering the growth of generated content in the web by machine learning

6

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

models[6][7] , it is not improbable that a proportion of the content collected and used by machine learning models is going to be biased by other models that are continuously generating content.

**The Effect of Test Set Construction:** We first examine the impact of composition bias on the test sets. Figure 9 shows the fitted scaling exponents for all our test sets. The plot suggests that the scaling powers for source-original test sets are drastically different from those of target-original test sets. This behavior is in direct contrast with language modeling setting (Kaplan et al., 2020) where it was observed that the evaluation on different test sets merely acted as a scaling penalty that only changed the multiplicative constants of the scaling law.

To elucidate this phenomenon further, in Figure 11 of the appendix, we compare the scaling trends for different source and target original test sets. Several observations are in order: Test sets with a similar composition approach (source or target original) have a qualitatively similar scaling behavior. However, scaling behavior is vastly different between the two composition approaches. Reducible loss quickly decays to zero for source original test sets. In fact, we observe that scaling our baseline 6L-6L model by a factor of 2 _._ 5 is sufficient for ensuring that reducible loss is below 0 _._ 05 for all source original test sets. In contrast, on target original test sets, the loss decays much more slowly with model size. For comparison, to ensure that reducible loss is below 0 _._ 05 for all target original test sets, we estimate that the baseline model has to be scaled up by a factor of 11.

Because of this behavior, the value of larger models in NMT is closely tied to their evaluation sets: On source original data, due to larger scaling exponents, even moderate increases in model size are sufficient for pushing the reducible loss close to zero. Hence, beyond a few hundred million parameters, there is no benefit in increasing the model size. In contrast, for target original data, which generally have smaller scaling exponents, large models are needed to push the reducible loss to zero.

**The Effect of Training Set Construction:** In this section, we briefly examine the role of _training data_ construction on the scaling behavior. To do this, we generate two En _→_ De datasets, that were not used in the previous experiments. One fully target original and another completely source original.

To generate the target original dataset, we compile a set of German documents from the web. Documents are screened to ensure the data is not machine generated. We use a Hybrid model (with 380M parameters) (Chen et al., 2018) to back-translate (BT) these documents to English. Similarly, for the source original data, we collect human generated English documents and (forward) translate them to German using a hybrid model (with approximately 327M parameters). Both datasets provide us with approximately 2.2 billion training examples. We mimic the experimental setup of Section 2.

Note that even though these datasets are not human generated, they reflect important aspects of training large NMT models. Many modern NMT datasets are harvested from the web and as a result, are contaminated with machine generated data. Moreover, many popular data augmentation algorithms such as Back Translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), sequence level distillation (Kim & Rush, 2016) and self training (He et al., 2020) purposefully add machine generated data into the training pipeline in order to take advantage of monolingual data.

Figure 6 describes the scaling behavior for models trained on target-original data. We observe that even though larger models are successful in reducing the training loss, they are unable to improve the test loss after roughly 400M parameters. Once this size threshold is exceeded, models overfit the training data and the test loss starts to deteriorate across all of our test sets. We hypothesize that this size threshold corresponds to the capacity of the original back-translation model. This assertion suggests that in order for back-translation to be beneficial for training large models, it has to be performed with a models with comparable capacity or higher. Although quite intriguing, we leave the verification of this hypothesis to future work.

Figure 7 paints another interesting picture for the models trained on the source-original data only, implying the target side having the composition bias, expected to be simpler, dull and not rich in its content, in short - not natural looking. As experiments suggest, even our smallest models are able to achieve extremely low loss values (roughly 0 _._ 16), with an apparent overfitting pattern. We believe the same phenomenon is also related to the "data simplification" effect seeked by non-autoregressive models in NMT (Zhou et al., 2021).

## 4 EVOLUTION OF GENERATION QUALITY

We examine the effects of scaling on the output quality as measured by BLEU score[8] . For this analysis, we focus on output generated via beam search (Wu et al., 2016). For tractability purposes, we do not attempt to tune the (many) hyper-parameters of beam-search for each model. Instead, we use the configuration optimized for the baseline model (listed in Appendix J) in all the decoding tasks.

Figure 8 presents the co-evolution of BLEU score and cross-entropy loss throughout the training for all of our models. Depending on the construction of the test sets, two different empirical behaviors emerge. On target-original test sets, larger models are able to improve (lower) the test loss. These improvements in the loss are accompanied with consistent improvements (increases) in BLEU score. In fact, we observe that a simple power law can capture the relationship between BLEU score and cross-entropy loss for high-quality models.[9] In contrast, on source-original test sets, this relationship is absent; larger models consistently achieve better test losses, however, beyond a certain threshold, BLEU scores begin to deteriorate. Figures 27 and 28 exhibit that this phenomenon is not due to over-training; the BLEU score gap between large and small models is persistent throughout training.

To ensure that this observation truly reflects the generation quality of the models (as opposed to potential biases of BLEU score), we repeat our analysis with BLEURT score (Sellam et al., 2020a;b). The results are presented in Appendix K. The results show that BLEURT scores closely mirror the behavior of BLEU scores with respect to model scaling.

A careful look at the left-subplots of Figures 8 brings up another interesting trend. At similar values of the test loss, encoder-scaled models result in better generation quality compared to decoder-scaled models. This findings agrees with previous work that relied on encoder-scaling when optimizing for BLEU and inference latency (Kasai et al., 2021). Whether these differences in the effects of encoder-scaling and decoder-scaling are caused by insufficient search algorithms, or just different model fits from different architectural priors is left to future work.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND LIMITATION

In this work we have attempted to quantify the evolution of model quality as a function of model capacity for encoder-decoder NMT models. While a univariate scaling law describing the crossentropy as a function of the total number of parameters in the model is insufficient, a bivariate law treating the number of encoder and decoder parameters as separate variables adequately describes the scaling behavior of these models under various scaling strategies. We validate this behavior on a variety of language pairs and evaluation sets. Whether this behavior is intrinsic to the encoder-decoder architecture, or arising from the nature of the NMT task, requires further study.

Next, we show that this scaling behavior is highly dependent on the composition of the evaluation data, specifically on whether the source or target sentences are “original”. Our findings indicate that target-original evaluation sets continue benefiting from model scaling, while the reducible error on source-original evaluation sets quickly saturates to 0. This could be an artifact of the lack of diversity in translated text; a simpler target distribution doesn’t require much capacity to model while generating fluent or natural-looking text could benefit much more from scale.

We also study how the composition of training data affects the scaling behavior of models. When training on target-original (back-translated) text, model quality keeps improving until a point after which the trend saturates. In our study the capacity where saturation manifests first is perilously close to the capacity of the model used for back-translation, indicating that the capacity of the generative model used to generate synthetic text might have a role to play, but this requires further investigation. When training on source-original text, even low-capacity models are sufficient to reach the irreducible loss region, painting a gloomy picture for synthetic data. While we have explored these ideas in the context of machine translation, given the proliferation of generative models this problem will likely be a challenge for future practitioners training on web-scraped monolingual datasets as well. For low-resource languages, the proliferation of machine translated text is already a problem given that a significant portion of web text in these languages is machine translated.

Finally, we attempt to understand how generation quality evolves with the improvements in crossentropy resulting from model scaling. As with our previous findings, dataset composition plays a major role in determining the trends. For source-original evaluation sets, the correlation between cross-entropy and generation quality breaks down. On target-original evaluation, we observe an inverse correlation between cross-entropy and BLEU, suggesting that improved model fit results in a corresponding improvement in generation quality. The slope of this relationship is different for encoder-scaling and decoder-scaling, with encoder-scaled models performing better on BLEU than decoder-scaled models, at the same level of cross-entropy loss. Whether this is an artifact of our search strategy or the difference in architectural priors is something that requires further investigation.

Our findings suggest that scaling behavior of encoder-decoder NMT models is predictable, but the exact formulation of scaling laws might vary depending on the particular architecture or task being studied. Our empirical findings also raise concerns regarding the effect of synthetic data on model scaling and evaluation, and how proliferation of machine generated text might hamper the quality of future models trained on web-text.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a valuable contribution by extending scaling law analysis to encoder-decoder NMT models, identifying critical limitations of univariate scaling laws, and highlighting the impact of data composition on model performance. Its empirical rigor, broad model scale, and multilingual evaluation are strengths. The finding that decoder scaling dominates loss reduction while encoder scaling benefits generation quality is both novel and practically relevant.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient control over architectural variables in depth scaling: The paper claims to isolate the effects of encoder and decoder depth, but keeps internal layer hyperparameters (model dimension, feed-forward width, attention heads) fixed across scales. This conflates depth effects with width-to-depth ratio changes, potentially confounding the observed scaling exponents. For example, shallow but wide models may behave differently than deep and narrow ones even at similar parameter counts. The authors acknowledge in Appendix H that small models show larger variation by scaling approach, suggesting shape matters—yet they dismiss this for large models based on only 1–2% loss differences. A more rigorous approach would involve controlled scaling (e.g., compound scaling that preserves aspect ratios) or at least an ablation showing that internal width does not interact with depth effects on exponents pe and pd.

- Limited language pair diversity risks overgeneralization: The study focuses exclusively on high-resource language pairs (En↔De, En↔Zh), all involving English, with similar syntactic alignment and data availability. This raises concerns about generalizability to low-resource or morphologically complex languages where scaling dynamics may differ. For instance, prior work suggests agglutinative languages benefit less from model scale due to sparsity. The paper’s central claim—that bivariate scaling laws hold across languages—is weakened by testing only on two language families. Including at least one low-resource or distant-language pair (e.g., En→Swahili or En→Finnish) would strengthen the universality claim and align with best practices in NMT research.

- Potential data contamination in training and evaluation sets: The paper uses in-house web-crawled training data and internal test sets without describing filtering procedures against benchmark leakage. Given that WMT test sets are widely used in training data (e.g., via back-translation), there is a risk that the evaluation sets—especially news-domain ones derived from WMT—overlap with training data, inflating performance and distorting scaling trends. While the authors differentiate source- and target-original sets, they do not report whether any filtering was applied to prevent duplication. This is particularly problematic for the back-translation experiments, where the 380M teacher model may have been exposed to similar data. Without evidence of contamination checks, the observed saturation at 400M parameters could reflect overfitting to data artifacts rather than model capacity limits.

- Inadequate reporting of training compute and hyperparameters: The paper omits key hyperparameters and compute metrics necessary for reproducibility and Chinchilla-style analysis. While batch size and dropout are reported, learning rate schedules, gradient clipping, and optimizer settings (beyond naming Adafactor) are missing. More critically, total training FLOPs are not provided, preventing comparison of scaling with compute rather than just parameter count. This limits the paper’s ability to position its findings relative to optimal compute-efficient scaling laws. The omission is especially notable given the authors’ access to TPUv3 days—precisely the infrastructure needed to measure FLOPs. Without this, it is unclear whether observed scaling exponents reflect parameter efficiency or training inefficiency.

- Overreliance on cross-entropy without validating its role as a proxy for quality: Although the paper investigates BLEU and BLEURT, it primarily uses cross-entropy loss to derive scaling laws and optimal capacity allocation. Yet Figure 8 shows that loss improvements do not translate to BLEU gains on source-original data—precisely the setting where most training data resides. This decoupling undermines the practical relevance of the proposed scaling law (Eq. 1) for real-world deployment. The authors note encoder-scaled models outperform decoder-scaled ones at the same loss, suggesting architectural priors affect generation quality independently of fit. This implies that optimizing for loss alone may lead to suboptimal models. A more impactful framing would derive scaling laws directly for generation quality or explicitly model the loss-quality gap as a function of data composition.

- No simulation or example showing bivariate law predicts real gains: The paper claims that its bivariate scaling law (Eq. 1) can guide optimal capacity allocation, yet it does not demonstrate this with a concrete prediction task. Readers need to see the law used prospectively: for example, fit the model on small-to-medium encoder-decoder configurations and predict the loss for a large asymmetric configuration (e.g., 32-layer encoder, 8-layer decoder), then compare to actual training results. Without such a demonstration, the claim of predictive accuracy remains retrospective and curve-fitting in nature. This is standard practice in scaling law papers—Kaplan et al. (2020) do this for LM scaling—and is necessary to establish the law as a tool for design, not just description.

- Missing worked example of optimal allocation in a real system: Proposition 1 gives a formula for optimal encoder-decoder parameter split based on exponents $p_e$ and $p_d$, but the paper never computes this for any language pair or test set. A reader cannot tell whether the optimal ratio is 1:2, 2:1, or more extreme. The paper should compute $N_e^*$ and $N_d^*$ for English→German using the fitted exponents from Figure 9 and report the resulting allocation. Then, it should train a model at that split and verify that it outperforms symmetric or encoder-heavy baselines in test loss. This would ground the theoretical result in practice and show its value for model design.

- No discussion of how to estimate scaling exponents in practice: The paper treats $p_e$ and $p_d$ as known quantities, but offers no guidance on how many models or what scaling paths are needed to estimate them reliably. For instance, how many encoder-scaled models are sufficient to pin down $p_e$ within 10% error? Is a grid of 5×5 encoder-decoder sizes necessary, or can a diagonal path suffice? Given the high cost of training (2700 TPUv3 days for largest models), practitioners need rules of thumb for efficient exponent estimation. The paper should include a small-scale ablation—e.g., on a low-resource language or smaller architecture—showing convergence of exponent estimates with sample size, or at least cite a rule from prior work and justify its use.

- Unverified claim about back-translation model capacity limiting scaling: The paper observes that performance saturates near 400M parameters when training on back-translated data and notes this is close to the 380M-parameter model used for back-translation. It hypothesizes that the teacher model's capacity bounds the achievable quality, but provides no test of this. To validate it, the authors could re-run the experiment using a larger back-translation model (e.g., 1B parameters) and check whether the saturation threshold shifts upward. Even a partial test—e.g., generating a smaller dataset with a larger teacher—would strengthen the claim. As it stands, the observation is intriguing but speculative, and the central policy implication (use large teachers for synthetic data) lacks empirical support.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Control for architectural shape effects by either conducting compound scaling experiments or demonstrating that internal width does not interact with depth in driving scaling exponents.

- Expand language pair diversity to include at least one low-resource or morphologically complex language to support generalizability claims.

- Report data contamination checks between training and evaluation sets, especially for WMT-derived test data, and detail filtering procedures.

- Include total training FLOPs and full optimizer settings to enable compute-based scaling analysis and improve reproducibility.

- Reframe the scaling law analysis to account for the loss-quality decoupling by either modeling BLEU/BLEURT directly or incorporating data composition as a moderating variable in the law.

comments.title: Predictive validation of scaling law missing
comments.quote: We propose a formula which describes the scaling behavior of cross-entropy loss as a bivariate function of encoder and decoder size, and show that it gives accurate predictions under a variety of scaling approaches and languages; we show that the total number of parameters alone is not sufficient for such purposes.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the proposed bivariate law provides accurate predictions, but the paper only fits the law to observed data without demonstrating prospective forecasting. To validate predictive power, the authors should fit the model on small or medium-sized configurations and predict loss for a held-out asymmetric or large model (e.g., 32-layer encoder, 8-layer decoder), then compare to actual training results. Without such a test, the claim remains descriptive rather than predictive. This is standard in scaling law studies (e.g., Kaplan et al., 2020) and necessary to establish the law as a design tool.

comments.title: Misleading phrasing of loss-BLEU divergence
comments.quote: For test sets which were originally translated from target language to source language, both loss and BLEU score improve as model size increases. In contrast, for test sets originally translated from source language to target language, the loss improves, but the BLEU score stops improving after a certain threshold.
comments.explanation: The phrasing incorrectly attributes the loss-BLEU divergence to translation direction rather than data origin. The real distinction lies in whether the target-side reference is naturally written (target-original) or machine-translated (source-original). The current wording suggests reversibility—e.g., that En→De vs De→En would behave differently—when the effect depends on target-side naturalness. This risks misinterpretation. The sentence should clarify that BLEU saturation occurs specifically when evaluating on source-original test sets, where the target side is synthetic.

comments.title: Ambiguous and misleading definition of composition bias
comments.quote: We also report that the scaling behavior of the model is acutely influenced by composition bias of the train/test sets, which we define as any deviation from naturally generated text (either via machine generated or human translated text).
comments.explanation: The definition conflates human-translated text with machine-generated content as sources of 'bias', but human translations are naturally produced linguistic artifacts, not synthetic. The intended contrast is between text originally authored in a language versus translated text (regardless of origin). By grouping human and machine translation under 'deviation from naturalness', the paper mischaracterizes the phenomenon. A clearer definition would distinguish between source-crawled and target-crawled test sets, or frame bias in terms of authorship rather than generation mode.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about absence of prior NMT scaling studies
comments.quote: Neural machine translation (NMT) has long enjoyed the benefits of scaling *(Huang et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Lepikhin et al., 2021)*, but studies investigating the scaling behavior of NMT models are missing.
comments.explanation: The claim that no prior work has studied NMT scaling behavior is inaccurate. Several studies—including Bhosale et al. (2021), Bansal et al. (2022), and Fernandes et al. (2023)—have explicitly analyzed scaling laws in NMT. While this paper offers a novel bivariate analysis separating encoder and decoder effects, it does not follow that prior scaling research is absent. This overstatement undermines scholarly accuracy. The sentence should be revised to acknowledge existing work and position the contribution as a refinement rather than a first.

comments.title: Inconsistent interpretation of scaling exponents and model benefit
comments.quote: Because of this behavior, the value of larger models in NMT is closely tied to their evaluation sets: On source original data, due to larger scaling exponents, even moderate increases in model size are sufficient for pushing the reducible loss close to zero. Hence, beyond a few hundred million parameters, there is no benefit in increasing the model size. In contrast, for target original data, which generally have smaller scaling exponents, large models are needed to push the reducible loss to zero.
comments.explanation: The logic is reversed: larger exponents imply faster improvement with scale, so target-original sets with *smaller* exponents should benefit *less* from scaling, not more. Yet the paper claims large models are needed for those sets. This contradiction arises from conflating exponent magnitude with total parameter requirement. While the empirical observation may hold, the explanation misaligns with scaling theory. The text should clarify that smaller exponents lead to diminishing returns, requiring disproportionately large models for marginal gains, rather than implying smaller exponents enable greater benefit.

comments.title: Overstated claim about reducible error saturation
comments.quote: Our findings indicate that target-original evaluation sets continue benefiting from model scaling, while the reducible error on source-original evaluation sets quickly saturates to 0.
comments.explanation: The claim that reducible error saturates to zero implies the model reaches the irreducible (Bayes) loss, but the paper provides no evidence for this—no oracle estimates, human agreement studies, or lower bounds on translation uncertainty. Saturation at a low level is not equivalent to reaching the theoretical limit. The observation may reflect data limitations, architectural constraints, or evaluation set artifacts rather than task optimality. The phrasing should be revised to 'saturates at a low level' or 'shows diminishing returns' to avoid overinterpreting empirical trends as theoretical convergence.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated extrapolation to low-resource languages
comments.quote: For low-resource languages, the proliferation of machine translated text is already a problem given that a significant portion of web text in these languages is machine translated.
comments.explanation: This claim extends beyond the paper’s empirical scope: all experiments use high-resource language pairs (En↔De, En↔Zh). No data from low-resource languages is presented, nor is there evidence supporting the asserted prevalence or impact of machine-translated text in those settings. While the concern may be valid, introducing it in the conclusion without empirical grounding risks overgeneralization. The statement should be removed or reframed as a hypothesis for future work, not a conclusion drawn from the current study.

comments.title: Contradiction between bivariate claim and optimal univariate reduction
comments.quote: While a univariate scaling law describing the cross-entropy as a function of the total number of parameters in the model is insufficient, a bivariate law treating the number of encoder and decoder parameters as separate variables adequately describes the scaling behavior of these models under various scaling strategies.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts Proposition 1, which shows that under optimal allocation—$N_e/N_d = p_e/p_d$—the bivariate law reduces to a univariate power law in total parameters $B$ with exponent $p_e + p_d$. Thus, univariate scaling *is* sufficient when capacity is allocated optimally. The paper should clarify that univariate laws fail under asymmetric scaling (e.g., encoder-only or decoder-only), but become adequate under proportional scaling, aligning the abstract with its own theoretical result.

comments.title: Scaling paths differ in maximum parameter capacity
comments.quote:
Encoder Scaling: vary encoder depth ($2$ to $64$) while decoder depth is fixed ($6$ layers).
Decoder Scaling: vary decoder depth ($2$ to $64$) while encoder depth is fixed ($6$ layers).
comments.explanation: The encoder and decoder scaling paths allow different maximum parameter counts: encoder scaling reaches ~1.43B (64×20M + 6×25M), while decoder scaling reaches ~1.72B (6×20M + 64×25M). This 290M parameter gap confounds architectural comparisons—better performance in decoder scaling could stem from higher capacity, not intrinsic efficiency. To isolate the effect of which component is scaled, the analysis should control for total parameter budget, either by matching final sizes or plotting loss against depth. Otherwise, the comparison is biased.

comments.title: Symmetric scaling includes lower-depth regimes than asymmetric paths
comments.quote: Symmetric Scaling: increasing decoder and encoder layers together (from $2$ to $64$), i.e. the number of Transformer Blocks in the encoder and decoder being equal.
comments.explanation: The symmetric path starts at 2+2 layers, while encoder/decoder scaling experiments fix the baseline at 6 layers. This means symmetric models include configurations (e.g., 2+2, 3+3) far below the depth regime of the asymmetric cases, introducing a lower-capacity regime not otherwise studied. This creates an unfair comparison: symmetric scaling appears to cover a broader range, but part of it operates at depths never tested in the asymmetric settings. The symmetric path should start from 6+6 to ensure depth comparability across conditions.

comments.title: Exclusion of embedding/softmax parameters distorts small-model scaling
comments.quote: Following the convention, we do not count the parameters in the embedding and softmax layers towards the model size.
comments.explanation: Embedding and softmax layers contribute ~65M parameters (32k × 1024 × 2), which is 81% of the reported minimum model size (80M). Excluding them significantly distorts the scaling trajectory at small sizes and may affect exponent estimation. While common in some studies, the paper provides no justification or sensitivity analysis. To ensure validity, the authors should either include these parameters or demonstrate their exclusion does not alter the scaling trends, especially in the low-parameter regime.

comments.title: Parameter count range does not support 50× scaling claim
comments.quote: We train $200$ such models which scale the model size by approximately a factor of $50$ (from $80$M parameters to $4$B parameters).
comments.explanation: The stated scaling factor is inconsistent with the architecture: minimum (2+2) gives ~90M, maximum symmetric (64+64) gives ~2.88B, a factor of ~32×, not 50×. Even under asymmetric scaling, the maximum is ~1.72B, still short of 4B. The discrepancy suggests either incorrect parameter estimates, unreported configurations, or a misstatement. The scaling factor should be corrected to reflect the actual range, or the architecture details clarified to justify the 4B figure.

comments.title: Multiplicative scaling law lacks mechanistic justification
comments.quote:
Then, our proposed scaling law has the form

$$
\hat {L} \left(N _ {e}, N _ {d}\right) = \alpha N _ {e} ^ {- p _ {e}} N _ {d} ^ {- p _ {d}} + L _ {\infty} \tag {1}
$$
comments.explanation: The multiplicative form assumes independent power-law reductions from encoder and decoder capacity, but no architectural or dynamical rationale is given for why the effects should combine multiplicatively rather than additively or via another coupling. Alternative forms—e.g., $\hat{L} = \alpha_e N_e^{-p_e} + \alpha_d N_d^{-p_d} + L_\infty$—could also capture asymmetric scaling. While the current form fits well, the absence of a comparison with alternatives (via AIC/BIC or cross-validation) weakens the claim of generality. The paper should include such a comparison in an appendix to justify the functional choice.

comments.title: Incorrect interpretation of α as maximum loss reduction
comments.quote: In this formulation,  $\alpha$  corresponds to the maximum loss reduction that one can hope from scaling, while  $p_e$  and  $p_d$  are the scaling exponents for encoder and decoder respectively.
comments.explanation: The parameter $\alpha$ cannot represent 'maximum loss reduction' because as $N_e \to 0$ or $N_d \to 0$, the reducible term $\alpha N_e^{-p_e} N_d^{-p_d} \to \infty$, making the initial loss unbounded. The actual reduction is $\lim_{N\to\infty} (\hat{L} - L_\infty) = 0$, so the total possible improvement depends on the baseline. $\alpha$ is instead a positive amplitude that scales the magnitude of the reducible component and is sensitive to normalization. It should be described as a fitted scaling constant, not a bound on achievable improvement.

comments.title: Claimed optimal allocation lacks practical demonstration
comments.quote:
ndix G.

Proposition 1 (Optimal Scaling). Assume the loss performance of the model is described by Eq. (1). Let  $B$  denote the budget for the total number of parameters. Then, the optimal encoder / decoder sizes (denoted respectively by  $N_{e}^{*}$  and  $N_{d}^{*}$ ) are  $N_{e}^{*} = \frac{p_{e}}{p_{e} + p_{d}} B$  and  $N_{d}^{*} = \frac{p_{d}}{p_{e} + p_{d}}
comments.explanation: The paper derives an optimal allocation formula but never computes it for any language pair or test set. Readers cannot determine whether the optimal split is balanced or extreme. To demonstrate utility, the authors should compute $N_e^*$ and $N_d^*$ for En→De using fitted exponents from Figure 9, train a model at that split, and verify it outperforms symmetric or imbalanced baselines. Without this, the result remains theoretical and disconnected from practice.

comments.title: No guidance on estimating scaling exponents in practice
comments.quote:
Then, our proposed scaling law has the form

$$
\hat {L} \left(N _ {e}, N _ {d}\right) = \alpha N _ {e} ^ {- p _ {e}} N _ {d} ^ {- p _ {d}} + L _ {\infty} \tag {1}
$$
comments.explanation: The paper treats $p_e$ and $p_d$ as known quantities but offers no guidance on how to estimate them efficiently. Given the high cost of training (2700 TPUv3 days), practitioners need rules of thumb: How many encoder-scaled models are needed? Is a full 5×5 grid required, or can diagonal paths suffice? A small-scale ablation showing exponent convergence with sample size, or citation of prior work on efficient estimation, would make the method actionable. As it stands, the approach is prohibitively expensive to apply without such guidance.

comments.title: Hypothesis about back-translation model capacity lacks empirical support
comments.quote: We hypothesize that this size threshold corresponds to the capacity of the original back-translation model. This assertion suggests that in order for back-translation to be beneficial for training large models, it has to be performed with a models with comparable capacity or higher.
comments.explanation: The hypothesis that overfitting begins when the student exceeds the teacher's capacity (380M → ~400M) is intriguing but speculative. If the student is larger, it should better model the teacher's output, not overfit to its artifacts—unless those artifacts are systematic and learnable, which is not shown. Overfitting could stem from data diversity, optimization, or architecture. Because the paper presents this as the primary explanation without acknowledging alternatives, it risks misleading readers. The claim should be reframed as an observation requiring validation, not a supported conclusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'larger models' in BLEU degradation
comments.quote: On source-original test sets, this relationship is absent; larger models consistently achieve better test losses, however, beyond a certain threshold, BLEU scores begin to deteriorate.
comments.explanation: The term 'larger models' is ambiguous—it could refer to total size, encoder depth, decoder depth, or training duration. Since the paper separately scales encoder and decoder, the claim must specify which scaling path causes BLEU degradation. Figure 8 does not label whether points correspond to encoder- or decoder-scaled models on source-original sets, so the reader cannot verify the effect. If only one direction causes the drop, the conclusion changes. The claim should clarify which models exhibit degradation or be revised to reflect the uncertainty.

comments.title: Decoder parameter difference lacks structural explanation
comments.quote: Each encoder layer adds approximately 20M parameters to the model while each decoder layer adds around 25M parameters.
comments.explanation: The paper fails to explain that the 5M difference arises from the decoder's cross-attention module, which is critical for understanding architectural asymmetry. Without this, readers may misattribute the gap to other factors. The sentence should explicitly state that the additional parameters come from cross-attention, e.g., 'Each decoder layer adds ~25M due to the additional cross-attention module.' This improves reproducibility and conceptual clarity.

comments.title: High R² claim lacks context on data points and overfitting risk
comments.quote: The results suggest that our scaling law almost fully captures the variation in the data  $(R^2 > 99\%)$ . In particular, in contrast to traditional scaling laws, our proposed formulation is able to capture the different scaling behaviors of encoder and decoder scaled models.
comments.explanation: An in-sample $R^2 > 99\%$ with four free parameters does not guarantee model adequacy without knowing the number of data points. If few configurations are used (e.g., <15), this may reflect overfitting. The paper does not report the number of points used in Figure 2 or compare model complexity to simpler alternatives. The stronger evidence comes from out-of-sample tests (Figures 3–4). The $R^2$ claim should be contextualized with sample size and reserved for out-of-sample evaluations when discussing model quality.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents an empirical study of scaling laws for neural machine translation, proposing a bivariate scaling law for encoder-decoder models. While the experiments are extensive and the proposed scaling law fits the data well, there are significant concerns about the generalizability of findings, the relationship between cross-entropy and translation quality, and the treatment of synthetic training data.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by being the first systematic study of scaling laws specifically for encoder-decoder NMT models. The proposed bivariate scaling law is well-motivated and empirically validated across multiple language pairs. The investigation into composition bias effects on scaling behavior is particularly insightful and timely given the proliferation of synthetic data on the web.

Overview issues:

- Limited Generalizability Beyond Depth Scaling: The paper acknowledges focusing primarily on depth scaling while keeping width fixed, but this limitation undermines the claimed generality of the scaling law. The authors report in Appendix H that for small models (<100M parameters), different scaling approaches show significant variation, yet they dismiss this as outside their scope. This is problematic because many practical NMT systems operate at these smaller scales. The scaling law's applicability to width scaling or mixed scaling strategies remains unverified, which limits its practical utility for model design decisions.

- Insufficient Analysis of BLEU-Cross-Entropy Disconnect: The paper observes that on source-original test sets, BLEU scores deteriorate despite improving cross-entropy loss beyond a certain threshold, but provides only speculative explanations. This disconnect between training objective and evaluation metric is a critical finding that warrants deeper investigation. The authors suggest it might relate to search algorithms or architectural priors but do not test these hypotheses. Without understanding why this breakdown occurs, practitioners cannot determine whether to trust cross-entropy improvements or adjust their training objectives for different data compositions.

- Back-Translation Capacity Hypothesis Unverified: The authors hypothesize that when training on back-translated data, model performance saturates at approximately the capacity of the back-translation model used (380M parameters). This is an important claim with practical implications for data augmentation strategies, but it remains untested. The paper would be significantly stronger if it included experiments varying the back-translation model capacity to verify this threshold effect. Without this validation, the recommendation to use higher-capacity models for back-translation lacks empirical support.

- Narrow Language Pair Selection: The study only examines four language pairs (En-De, De-En, En-Zh, Zh-En), all involving English and either German or Chinese. This limited selection raises concerns about whether the observed scaling behaviors generalize to other language families, particularly morphologically rich languages or distant language pairs. The paper's claims about universal scaling properties for NMT would be more convincing with inclusion of at least one language pair from a different family (e.g., Romance, Slavic, or low-resource languages) to demonstrate robustness across typological diversity.

- Fixed Decoding Parameters Across Model Scales: The authors use beam search parameters optimized for the baseline model across all model sizes, acknowledging they do not tune decoding hyperparameters for each scale. This methodological choice could artificially suppress the BLEU improvements of larger models, particularly on source-original data where the BLEU-entropy relationship breaks down. Larger models might benefit from different beam widths or length penalties, and not optimizing these parameters may lead to underestimation of the true generation quality gains from scaling.

- Missing demonstration of scaling law necessity: The paper proposes a bivariate scaling law but never demonstrates that simpler alternatives fail. To establish that the bivariate form is truly necessary, the authors should include a systematic comparison against univariate laws using the same data. They could fit the traditional power law L(N) = αN^(-p) + L∞ to their encoder/decoder scaling data and show quantitatively how it fails to predict out-of-sample performance on symmetrically scaled models. This would directly motivate why the more complex bivariate form is needed rather than just asserting it.

- No worked example of optimal parameter allocation: Proposition 1 provides a formula for optimal encoder/decoder allocation, but the paper never walks through a concrete calculation. Readers would benefit from seeing the optimal allocation computed for a specific budget (e.g., 1B parameters) using the fitted exponents from Figure 9, then comparing the predicted loss against both symmetric scaling and the empirical results. This worked example would demonstrate the practical utility of their theoretical result and help practitioners apply it to real model design decisions.

- Insufficient validation of BLEU-entropy relationship: The paper observes different BLEU-entropy relationships for source-original vs target-original data but only shows this qualitatively. To make this finding robust, they should quantify the relationship by fitting separate power-law models for each data composition and reporting confidence intervals. Additionally, they should test whether the relationship holds on held-out test sets beyond those shown in Figure 8. Without statistical validation and out-of-sample testing, the claim about composition-dependent BLEU-entropy relationships remains suggestive rather than conclusive.

- Missing guidance for practical implementation: The paper derives scaling laws but provides minimal guidance for practitioners. For instance, they should discuss how to estimate the scaling exponents (p_e, p_d) for new language pairs with limited compute budget, or provide recommendations on the minimum number of scaling experiments needed to reliably fit the bivariate law. They could include a practical guide showing how to extrapolate performance from a few small-scale training runs to predict large-scale behavior, which would make their findings more actionable for researchers with limited resources.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Expand experiments to include width scaling and mixed scaling strategies to validate the bivariate scaling law's generality across different parameter allocation schemes

- Conduct targeted experiments to verify the back-translation capacity hypothesis by training models with BT data generated from different capacity models

- Add at least one language pair from a different language family (e.g., Romance or Slavic) to demonstrate the scaling laws' robustness across typological diversity

- Investigate the BLEU-entropy disconnect on source-original data through controlled experiments varying decoding strategies and analyzing error patterns

- Include small-scale model results (<100M parameters) in the main analysis rather than relegating them to an appendix, given their practical relevance

comments.title: Parameter count inconsistency in scaling description
comments.quote: Each encoder layer adds approximately 20M parameters to the model while each decoder layer adds around 25M parameters. We train $200$ such models which scale the model size by approximately a factor of $50$ (from $80$M parameters to $4$B parameters).
comments.explanation: The parameter counts don't add up consistently with the stated scaling range. If each encoder layer adds ~20M parameters and each decoder layer adds ~25M parameters, and the smallest model starts at 80M parameters, let's verify the scaling: With symmetric scaling from 2 to 64 layers, the parameter increase would be (64-2) × (20M + 25M) = 62 × 45M = 2.79B parameters. Adding this to the base 80M gives ~2.87B parameters, not 4B. Similarly, for encoder-only scaling from 2 to 64 layers with fixed 6-layer decoder: (64-2) × 20M = 1.24B + base parameters ≈ 1.32B parameters. The claimed 4B maximum seems inconsistent with the per-layer parameter additions. The authors should either correct the per-layer parameter estimates or adjust the stated maximum model size to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Inconsistent layer count ranges across scaling approaches
comments.quote:
Encoder Scaling: vary encoder depth ($2$ to $64$) while decoder depth is fixed ($6$ layers).
Decoder Scaling: vary decoder depth ($2$ to $64$) while encoder depth is fixed ($6$ layers).
Symmetric Scaling: increasing decoder and encoder layers together (from $2$ to $64$), i.e. the number of Transformer Blocks in the encoder and decoder being equal.
comments.explanation: The layer count ranges create an inconsistency in the experimental design. For encoder scaling, the minimum configuration has 2 encoder + 6 decoder = 8 total layers. For decoder scaling, it's 6 encoder + 2 decoder = 8 total layers. But for symmetric scaling, the minimum is 2 encoder + 2 decoder = 4 total layers. This means the symmetric scaling experiments start from a fundamentally different architecture size than the asymmetric scaling experiments. Since the paper claims to study scaling behavior, this inconsistency could confound the results. The symmetric scaling should start from 6+6 layers to match the baseline used in asymmetric scaling, or the authors should acknowledge this difference and its potential impact on the scaling comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of N in power law fitting
comments.quote: To compare the empirical results with the scaling laws present in the literature for decoder only models *(Kaplan et al., 2020; Henighan et al., 2020)*, we have fitted a power law of the form $\hat{L}(N)=\alpha N^{-p}+L_{\infty}$ to the data. Here, $N$ is the total number of parameters outside of embedding / softmax layers and $\{\alpha,p,L_{\infty}\}$ are fitted parameters.
comments.explanation: The definition of $N$ as 'the total number of parameters outside of embedding / softmax layers' creates ambiguity when fitting the traditional power law to encoder-only and decoder-only scaling experiments. For encoder scaling experiments, it's unclear whether $N$ should include only the varying encoder parameters or both encoder and decoder parameters (where decoder is fixed). Similarly for decoder scaling experiments. This ambiguity affects the validity of the claim that traditional power laws fail to capture the scaling behavior, as the fitting procedure may not be consistent with how $N$ is defined in the cited literature where $N$ typically represents all model parameters. Rewrite the definition as 'Here, $N$ is the total number of non-embedding parameters in the model (including both encoder and decoder components)' and clarify how $N$ is computed for each scaling experiment.

comments.title: Unverified back-translation capacity hypothesis
comments.quote: We hypothesize that this size threshold corresponds to the capacity of the original back-translation model. This assertion suggests that in order for back-translation to be beneficial for training large models, it has to be performed with a models with comparable capacity or higher. Although quite intriguing, we leave the verification of this hypothesis to future work.
comments.explanation: The hypothesis that performance saturates at the back-translation model capacity (380M parameters) is presented as an explanation for the observed threshold at 400M parameters. However, this claim lacks empirical support in the current experiments. The authors use a 380M parameter BT model and observe saturation around 400M, but this single data point cannot establish the claimed relationship. To verify this hypothesis, the authors should have tested with BT models of different capacities (e.g., 200M, 600M parameters) to demonstrate that the saturation threshold scales with BT model capacity. Without this validation, the hypothesis remains speculative.

comments.title: Contradiction with Proposition 1 optimal scaling claim
comments.quote: Our results indicate that the scaling behavior is largely determined by the total capacity of the model, and the capacity allocation between the encoder and the decoder.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts Proposition 1 in Section 2.3, which shows that optimal scaling requires a specific ratio N_e/N_d = p_e/p_d, not just any allocation. Proposition 1 demonstrates that the allocation between encoder and decoder is critical for achieving optimal scaling behavior, with the scaling law reducing to L_opt(B) = α*B^(-(p_d + p_e)) + L_∞ only when optimally allocated. The introduction's claim that scaling is 'largely determined by total capacity' understates the importance of proper allocation. The text should be revised to emphasize that both total capacity AND proper allocation between encoder/decoder are essential for optimal scaling.

comments.title: Contradiction with Section 2.3 on decoder scaling effectiveness
comments.quote: At similar values of the test loss, encoder-scaled models result in better generation quality compared to decoder-scaled models. This findings agrees with previous work that relied on encoder-scaling when optimizing for BLEU and inference latency (Kasai et al., 2021).
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the main finding in Section 2.3, which states that 'when improving the test loss is concerned, it is much more effective to scale the decoder rather than the encoder.' The contradiction is significant because Section 2.3 presents this as a key insight that challenges conventional practice, while Section 4 suggests encoder scaling is actually better for generation quality. The authors need to reconcile these opposing claims - either by explaining why decoder scaling is better for loss reduction but encoder scaling is better for generation quality at the same loss level, or by acknowledging the inconsistency. As written, readers are left with contradictory guidance about optimal scaling strategies.

comments.title: Missing justification for Proposition 1 optimality
comments.quote: Proposition 1 (Optimal Scaling). Assume the loss performance of the model is described by Eq. (1). Let  $B$  denote the budget for the total number of parameters. Then, the optimal encoder / decoder sizes (denoted respectively by  $N_{e}^{*}$  and  $N_{d}^{*}$ ) are  $N_{e}^{*} = \frac{p_{e}}{p_{e} + p_{d}} B$  and  $N_{d}^{*} = \frac{p_{d}}{p_{e} + p_{d}} B$ . In addition, when optimally scaling the model, the scaling law reduces to  $L_{opt}(B) = \alpha^{*}B^{-(p_{d} + p_{e})} + L_{\infty}$ , where  $\alpha^{*} \equiv \alpha (\frac{p_{e} + p_{d}}{p_{e}})^{p_{e}}(\frac{p_{e} + p_{d}}{p_{d}})^{p_{d}}$ .
comments.explanation: The proposition claims optimality but doesn't specify the optimization problem being solved. The derivation requires minimizing L(N_e, N_d) subject to N_e + N_d = B, but this constraint formulation is not stated. Without explicitly defining the optimization problem, the claim of optimality is incomplete. Add 'We minimize the loss L(N_e, N_d) = αN_e^{-p_e}N_d^{-p_d} + L_∞ subject to the constraint N_e + N_d = B using Lagrange multipliers' before presenting the solution to address the missing justification.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about power law relationship
comments.quote: In fact, we observe that a simple power law can capture the relationship between BLEU score and cross-entropy loss for high-quality models.
comments.explanation: The paper claims a power law relationship exists but provides no mathematical formulation, fitted parameters, or quantitative evidence of goodness-of-fit. The dashed lines in Figure 8 are mentioned as power-law fits, but the text doesn't specify the functional form (e.g., BLEU = a * L^b + c) or report the fitted exponents. Without these details, readers cannot evaluate whether the claimed relationship is meaningful or assess its predictive power. The authors should either provide the specific power law equation and fitted parameters, or remove this claim as unsupported speculation.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset size reporting
comments.quote:
### Language Pairs

We report results on four language pairs: English$\rightarrow$German, German$\rightarrow$English, English$\rightarrow$Chinese, and Chinese$\rightarrow$English. We use in-house web-crawled training datasets with around $2.2$ billion sentence pairs (approximately $55$ billion tokens) for English$\leftrightarrow$German and 781 million sentence pairs for English$\leftrightarrow$Chinese.
comments.explanation: The dataset size reporting is inconsistent and potentially misleading. The text states 'around $2.2$ billion sentence pairs... for English$\rightarrow$German' but this appears to be for the English$\rightarrow$German direction only, not the bidirectional English$\leftrightarrow$German pair. Later it mentions '781 million sentence pairs for English$\leftrightarrow$Chinese' without clarifying if this is bidirectional or unidirectional. Since the paper studies four directional pairs (En→De, De→En, En→Zh, Zh→En), the dataset sizes should be reported per direction to match the experimental setup. Rewrite as: 'We use in-house web-crawled training datasets with around 2.2 billion sentence pairs (approximately 55 billion tokens) for English$\rightarrow$German and 2.2 billion sentence pairs for German$\rightarrow$English, and 781 million sentence pairs for English$\rightarrow$Chinese and 781 million sentence pairs for Chinese$\rightarrow$English.'

comments.title: Ambiguous model size definition excludes embeddings
comments.quote: Following the convention, we do not count the parameters in the embedding and softmax layers towards the model size.
comments.explanation: This convention creates ambiguity when comparing to other work and when calculating total computational requirements. The embedding and softmax layers can contribute significantly to parameter count, especially for large vocabularies common in NMT. For a vocabulary of 32k tokens and model dimension 1024, the embedding matrix alone would contain ~33M parameters, and the softmax layer another ~33M parameters. This exclusion could explain the discrepancy between the stated parameter counts and what readers might expect. The authors should clarify whether this exclusion affects their scaling law derivation, and if so, how the law accounts for the fixed embedding parameters that don't scale with model depth.

comments.title: Ambiguous test set directionality
comments.quote: For test sets which were originally translated from target language to source language, both loss and BLEU score improve as model size increases. In contrast, for test sets originally translated from source language to target language, the loss improves, but the BLEU score stops improving after a certain threshold.
comments.explanation: The abstract uses confusing terminology to describe test set origins. 'Originally translated from target language to source language' could mean either (a) human translations from target→source, or (b) machine translations from target→source. Given the paper's focus on composition bias, this distinction matters. The cross-reference to section 4 mentions 'source-original' vs 'target-original' test sets, which is clearer. Replace the quoted text with 'For target-original test sets (originally human-translated from target to source), both loss and BLEU score improve... In contrast, for source-original test sets (originally human-translated from source to target)...' because the current phrasing creates ambiguity about what constitutes the test data.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence about scaling exponent
comments.quote: Inspection of the functional form of Eq. (1) suggests that as long as  $N_{d} / N_{e}$  is fixed as the model scales (i.e. encoder and decoder grow proportionally together), the optimal scaling exponent,  $(p_e + p_d)$ , can
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically incomplete, ending with 'can' without completing the predicate. Based on the mathematical context and the subsequent discussion about achieving optimal scaling, the sentence should complete to 'can be achieved'. This creates a logical gap in the argument about proportional scaling. Rewrite the quoted text as 'Inspection of the functional form of Eq. (1) suggests that as long as  $N_{d} / N_{e}$  is fixed as the model scales (i.e. encoder and decoder grow proportionally together), the optimal scaling exponent,  $(p_e + p_d)$ , can be achieved' because the incomplete sentence creates a logical gap in the argument about proportional scaling.

comments.title: Undefined multiplicative constant α#
comments.quote: be achieved, albeit with a potentially sub-optimal multiplicative constant,  $\alpha^{\#}$ .
comments.explanation: The text introduces α# as a potentially sub-optimal multiplicative constant but never defines it mathematically or explains how it relates to the optimal constant α*. This creates ambiguity in the comparison between optimal and proportional scaling schemes. Add 'where $\alpha^{\#} = \alpha (\frac{N_e + N_d}{N_e})^{p_e}(\frac{N_e + N_d}{N_d})^{p_d}$ for the fixed ratio N_d/N_e' after the quoted text to define the sub-optimal constant.

comments.title: Missing definition of reducible loss
comments.quote: Reducible loss quickly decays to zero for source original test sets. In fact, we observe that scaling our baseline 6L-6L model by a factor of $2.5$ is sufficient for ensuring that reducible loss is below $0.05$ for all source original test sets.
comments.explanation: The term 'reducible loss' is used throughout this section without being defined. Based on the scaling law framework from Section 2.3, this likely refers to L(N) - L∞, but this should be explicitly stated. Without a clear definition, readers cannot interpret whether the 0.05 threshold is meaningful or how it relates to the overall loss values. The authors should define reducible loss at its first use in this section.

comments.title: Grammar error in 'This findings agrees'
comments.quote: This findings agrees with previous work that relied on encoder-scaling when optimizing for BLEU and inference latency (Kasai et al., 2021).
comments.explanation: The subject 'findings' is plural but the verb 'agrees' is singular. This should be 'This finding agrees' or 'These findings agree'. While minor, this grammatical error should be corrected for clarity and professionalism.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper presents an empirical investigation into scaling laws for encoder-decoder Transformer models in NMT, arguing that a bivariate scaling law based on encoder and decoder parameters is superior to a univariate law based on total parameter count. It also explores the impact of dataset composition (source-original vs. target-original) on scaling behavior and generation quality metrics.
